title: Sebastian Shaw
content:
{{Top|rwp|ffa}} {{Person |image=[[File:Sebastian_Shaw_1983.jpg]] |name=Sebastian Lewis Shaw |birth=[[May 29]], [[1900s|1905]]
[[Wikipedia:Holt, Norfolk|Holt]], [[Wikipedia:Norfolk|Norfolk]], [[Wikipedia:England|England]]Leech, Richard. "Better Than Beefcake: Sebastian Shaw," [[Wikipedia:The Guardian|''The Guardian'']], December 29, 1994, Features (section), pg. T12. |death=[[December 23]], [[1994]]
[[Wikipedia:Brighton|Brighton]], [[Wikipedia:East Sussex|East Sussex]], [[Wikipedia:England|England]]"Sebastian Shaw Obituary," [[Wikipedia:The Daily Telegraph|''The Daily Telegraph'']], January 2, 1995. |listedpronouns= |nationality=[[Wikipedia:English people|British]] |occupation=*[[Wikipedia:Actor|Actor]] *[[Wikipedia:Director|Director]] *[[Wikipedia:Novel|Novelist]] *[[Wikipedia:Playwright|Playwright]] *[[Wikipedia:Poet|Poet]] |companies= |website= }} {{Quote|Sebastian Shaw was the kind of character actor who could rise to any dramatic occasion. Whether in films, plays, television or on the radio, his unobtrusive manner, sharp intelligence, tactful style and polished technique gave all his work a quiet distinction.|Adan Benedick|{{WebCite|url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/people/sebastian-shaw-1572845.html|text=Sebastian Shaw|archivedate=20240213075929}}}} '''Sebastian Shaw''' ([[May 29]], [[1900s|1905]]–[[December 23]], [[1994]]) was an [[Wikipedia:English people|English]] [[Wikipedia:Stage (theatre)|stage]], [[Wikipedia:Film|film]], [[Wikipedia:Television|television]] and [[Wikipedia:Radio|radio]] [[Wikipedia:Actor|actor]], as well as a [[Wikipedia:Director|director]], [[Wikipedia:Novel|novelist]], [[Wikipedia:Playwright|playwright]] and [[Wikipedia:Poet|poet]]. Over the course of his 65-year career, Shaw appeared in more than 40 film and television productions, but was mostly known for his dozens of stage performances. A self-described "rotten actor" as a youth, Sebastian claimed to mature as a performer after returning from service in the [[Wikipedia:Royal Air Force|Royal Air Force]] during [[Wikipedia:World War II|World War II]]. Shaw was particularly known for his performances in [[Wikipedia:William Shakespeare|William Shakespeare]] productions, which were considered daring and ahead of his time. He also wrote a novel, ''The Christening'', in [[1975]]. One of Shaw's more famous roles is his brief but important performance in [[Star Wars: Episode VI Return of the Jedi|''Return of the Jedi'']], in which he portrayed a redeemed [[Anakin Skywalker]] during the moment aboard the [[DS-2 Death Star II Mobile Battle Station|Death Star II]] when [[Luke Skywalker]] unmasks his dying father. Due to the extreme secrecy demanded by ''[[Star Wars]]'' creator [[George Lucas]], Shaw knew no details of his part until he was guaranteed to play the role, and was forbidden from discussing it with anyone, even his family and friends, until after the theatrical release. Sebastian Shaw died of natural causes at age 89 in [[1994]]. Ten years later, his image as a [[Force ghost/Legends|Force ghost]] during the final scene of ''Return of the Jedi'' was replaced with that of [[Prequel trilogy|prequel]] actor [[Hayden Christensen]] for the movie's [[2004]] [[Wikipedia:DVD|DVD]] re-release. ==Biography== ===Early life=== Sebastian Lewis Shaw was born at [[Wikipedia:Holt, Norfolk|Holt]], [[Wikipedia:Norfolk|Norfolk]] in [[Wikipedia:England|England]] on [[May 29]], [[1900s|1905]]. His father, Dr. Geoffrey Shaw, was the [[Wikipedia:Music education|music master]] at [[Wikipedia:Gresham's School|Gresham's School]], a [[Wikipedia:North Norfolk|North Norfolk]] [[Wikipedia:Independent school|independent]] [[Wikipedia:Coeducational|coeducational]] [[Wikipedia:Boarding school|boarding school]], where Sebastian Shaw began his education. ===''Return of the Jedi''=== ====Casting==== {{Quote|I've no idea why they fixed on me. The point was, they decided that they needed a very experienced actor to play that very difficult scene. It wasn't easy to bring off.|Sebastian Shaw|{{WebCite|url=http://www.sebastianshaw.com/html/starlog.html|text=Starlog|archivedate=20080828003407|nolive=1}}}} [[File:Anakinredeemed.png|thumb|right|200px|Sebastian Shaw and [[Mark Hamill]] during their [[Star Wars: Episode VI Return of the Jedi|''Return of the Jedi'']] scene.]] In [[1982]], Sebastian Shaw was chosen for the brief, but crucial role as [[Anakin Skywalker]] in [[Star Wars: Episode VI Return of the Jedi|''Return of the Jedi'']], the third and final movie in the [[Original trilogy|original Star Wars trilogy]]. Although [[David Prowse]] played the costumed scenes and [[James Earl Jones]] portrayed the voice of Darth Vader, just as they had in the first two films, Shaw was to play a single scene with [[Mark Hamill]], during the moment aboard the [[DS-2 Death Star II Mobile Battle Station|Death Star]] when [[Luke Skywalker]] unmasks his dying father. Since the scene was arguably the emotional climax of the film, the casting crew wanted an experienced actor for the role. At the time he was approached for the film, Shaw was playing such Shakespearean roles as [[Wikipedia:Charles VI of France|Charles VI]] in [[Wikipedia:Henry V (play)|''Henry V'']] and [[Wikipedia:The Gravediggers|First Gravedigger]] in ''Hamlet'' at London's [[Wikipedia:Barbican Centre|Barbican Centre]]. Shaw was familiar with the previous two ''[[Star Wars]]'' films and enjoyed them particularly for the visual effects, which he described in an interview with [[Wikipedia:Science-fiction film|science-fiction film]] [[Wikipedia:Magazine|magazine]] [[Wikipedia:Starlog|''Starlog'']] as "brilliant techniques which, in many ways, were revolutionary, something quite new."Pirani, Adam. "Sebastian Shaw: The Return of Anakin Skywalker," [[Wikipedia:Starlog|''Starlog'']], July 1987, Vol. 11, Iss. 120, pg. 56-57,+96. ====Secrecy==== {{Quote|So secret was it that my actual contract said that I was not to disclose my role to nobody, not even my nearest and dearest.|Sebastian Shaw|}} Shaw participated in an [[Wikipedia:Audition|audition]] for the ''Return of the Jedi'' part but, due to the secrecy regarding the production, was not allowed to see his portion of the actual script until he had guaranteed to take the role and promised not to reveal any film secrets to anyone. His contract included a stipulation that he could not even tell family or friends the part he would be playing; Shaw told ''Starlog'' when asked by his loved ones about the role, he would answer, "I'm sorry, I'm not at liberty to tell you." Shaw received many [[Wikipedia:Journalism|press]] inquiries about his part in the movie once word of his casting was leaked, but he did not discuss his participation in the film until [[George Lucas]] gave him permission after the film's [[1983]] theatrical release. When Shaw arrived at the set for filming, he ran into his friend [[Ian McDiarmid]], the actor playing [[Emperor/Legends|Emperor]] [[Darth Sidious|Palpatine]], but Shaw did not tell him anything about the part. When McDiarmid asked him what he was doing there, Shaw simply responded, "I don't know, dear boy, I think it's something to do with science-fiction."{{InsiderCite|37|Something Wicked This Way Comes}} McDiarmid later reflected upon the comment, "That was Sebastian. The ''Star Wars'' saga had passed him blissfully by." ====Filming==== {{Quote|Incidentally, Mark nearly took my ears with it. 'Wow,' I said.|Sebastian Shaw, about when [[Mark Hamill]] removed the Darth Vader mask|}} [[File:Spirits_copy1.jpg|thumb|left|200px|Sebastian Shaw portraying [[Anakin Skywalker]] as a [[Force ghost/Legends|Force ghost]] alongside [[Yoda]] and [[Obi-Wan Kenobi]] during the original final scene of [[Star Wars: Episode VI Return of the Jedi|''Return of the Jedi'']].]] Shaw's scene in ''Return of the Jedi'' was filmed in only one day at [[Wikipedia:Elstree Studios|Elstree Studios]] in [[Wikipedia:Hertfordshire|Hertfordshire]], England. His presence during the filming was kept secret from all but the absolute minimum cast and crew. Director [[Richard Marquand]] directed the scene, but George Lucas was present for the entire length of filming. Shaw found the mask costume to be physically challenging during his scene; he had difficulty breathing through the two small holes in the quarter-inch thick Darth Vader mask that covered his entire face and head. His head was shaven, white make-up was applied to his face, black make-up was applied around his eyes and fake burn scars were applied to his head and cheeks. Regarding the screenplay by Lucas and [[Lawrence Kasdan]], Shaw told ''Starlog'' his unmasking scene "was so beautifully written that we never altered the dialogue at all." The scene was filmed in only a few takes, although some of Mark Hamill's [[Wikipedia:Close-up|close-ups]] took multiple takes because Shaw said "he was so terribly anxious to get it right." Shaw said the young actor was enjoyable to work with and appreciative of Shaw's experience. The unmasking scene lasts two minutes and seven seconds and includes twenty-four words of dialogue spoken by Shaw.{{Film|VI}} George Lucas personally directed Shaw for his appearance in the final scene of the movie, in which he is a [[Force ghost/Legends|Force ghost]] of Anakin Skywalker dressed in brown and white robes, his prior burns and injuries completely healed. The footage was shot against a [[Wikipedia:Chroma key|green screen]] and the characters of [[Obi-Wan Kenobi]] and [[Yoda]], with whom Anakin exchanges glances and smiles, were added later. Shaw did not know the purpose of the footage and believed it to be [[Wikipedia:Publicity|publicity]], not for the film. When directing the scene, Lucas simply advised the actor, "Look happy, smile." ====Response==== {{Quote|I was something of a movie star when I was young, before the great war, but I've never had so much fan mail as I've had from this film.|Sebastian Shaw|}} Shaw received more fan mail and autograph requests from ''Return of the Jedi'' than he had for any role in his career; he said most of the letters he received were from boys between the ages of twelve and fourteen, but he also heard from older people who were unexpectedly touched by the film. Shaw later reflected that he very much enjoyed his experience filming for ''Return of the Jedi'' and expressed particular surprise that an action figure was made of him from the film. ===Death=== {{Quote|He was a breathtaking phenomenon.|Richard Leech|}} Sebastian Shaw died of natural causes on [[December 23]], [[1994]] at age 89 in [[Wikipedia:Brighton|Brighton]], [[Wikipedia:East Sussex|East Sussex]], England. A funeral service was held [[February 15]], [[1995]] at [[Wikipedia:St Paul's, Covent Garden|St Paul's, Covent Garden]], commonly known as The Actors' Church due to its long association with the theatre community, in [[Wikipedia:Covent Garden|Covent Garden]], London. Actors Ian Richardson and [[Wikipedia:Ben Kingsley|Ben Kingsley]] read works by William Shakespeare; stage actress Estelle Kohler read ''The Ways of Love'' by [[Wikipedia:Victorian era|Victorian]] poet [[Wikipedia:Elizabeth Barrett Browning|Elizabeth Barrett Browning]]; actress [[Wikipedia:Sheila Allen|Sheila Allen]] read ''Life'' by [[Wikipedia:Wales|Welsh]] poet [[Wikipedia:George Herbert|George Herbert]]; and actor [[Wikipedia:Kenneth Branagh|Kenneth Branagh]] read from the works of [[Wikipedia:Canon (priest)|Canon]] [[Wikipedia:Henry Scott Holland|Henry Scott Holland]]. One of Shaw's own poems, ''Gemini'', was also read. [[Wikipedia:Baritone|Baritone]] Stephen Varcoe sang ''Wie bist du meine Konigin'' by [[Wikipedia:Johannes Brahms|Johannes Brahms]], accompanied by [[Wikipedia:Graham Johnson|Graham Johnson]] on the [[Wikipedia:Piano|piano]], and guitarist Martin Best performed and sang his composition of ''Ariel's Songs'' from [[Wikipedia:The Tempest|''The Tempest'']]. Shaw was survived by his partner [[Wikipedia:Joan Ingpen|Joan Ingpen]], daughter Drusilla MacLeod, sisters Susan Bonner-Morgan and Penelope Harness, and sister-in-law Olga Young."IN MEMORIAM Mr. Sebastian Shaw," [[Wikipedia:The Daily Telegraph|''The Daily Telegraph'']], February 15, 1995. Retrieved on 2008-08-01. ===''Return of the Jedi'' re-release changes=== {{Quote|The change didn't make any sense, any more than if George Lucas had replaced Sir Alec Guinness with Ewan McGregor. I didn't mind all the other little tweaks, but this left me feeling cheated. More than that, don't you think it's disrespectful to Sebastian Shaw?|Jimmy Jacobs, ''[[Star Wars]]'' fan, in a letter to film critic [[Wikipedia:Roger Ebert|Roger Ebert]]|[[Wikipedia:Roger Ebert|Ebert, Roger]]. "Anakin's fans strike back," [[Wikipedia:Chicago Sun-Times|''Chicago Sun-Times'']], May 1, 2005, Sunday Showcase (section), pg. 3.}} [[File:JediGhosts-ROTJ.png|thumb|right|200px|Sebastian Shaw's image was replaced with that of [[Hayden Christensen]] during the final scene of [[Star Wars: Episode VI Return of the Jedi|''Return of the Jedi'']], in the [[2004]] [[Wikipedia:DVD|DVD]] edition.]] In the [[2004]] [[Wikipedia:DVD|DVD]] edition of ''Return of the Jedi'', several changes and modifications were made to the scenes involving Sebastian Shaw. The unmasking scene with Mark Hamill remained mostly intact, but Shaw's eyebrows were digitally removed from his face due to the fact that they would be burned off Anakin Skywalker in the [[2005]] [[Prequel trilogy|prequel]], [[Star Wars: Episode III Revenge of the Sith|''Revenge of the Sith'']]. Shaw's eyes were also digitally colored to look more like those of [[Hayden Christensen]], who played Anakin in the prequel films. A far more drastic change, however, was the replacement of Shaw's image with that of Christensen during his appearance as a Force ghost in the last scene of ''Return of the Jedi''. This attempt to tie the prequel and [[Original trilogy|original]] trilogies together proved to be one of the least popular changes in the ''Star Wars'' re-releases.Johnson, Derek. "Star Wars fans, DVD, and cultural ownership: an interview with Will Brooker; Interview," [[Wikipedia:Velvet Light Trap|''Velvet Light Trap'']], September 22, 2005, pg. 36-44. Others, however, defended the move; although author and pop culture expert Will Brooker said George Lucas "totally Stalinized" Shaw by removing him from the scene, Brooker also disputed a claim that Luke Skywalker would not have recognized the young Anakin, explaining his connection to [[the Force]] allowed him to do so. Film reviewer [[Wikipedia:Roger Ebert|Roger Ebert]] described the change as "actually a little poetic" because it meant Anakin became one with the Force before he turned into Darth Vader. ==Works== ===Filmography=== {| {{Prettytable}} ! Year !! Title !! Contribution(s) !! Notes |- |[[1983]]||[[Star Wars: Episode VI Return of the Jedi|''Star Wars'': Episode VI ''Return of the Jedi'']]||[[Anakin Skywalker]]||In 2004, [[Hayden Christensen]]'s Force ghost replaced Shaw's at the end of the movie. |} ==Sources== *{{InsiderCite|28|Star News}} *{{GalaxyCite|11|Dark Father}} *{{GalaxyCite|11|Use the Force|Use the Force: Darth Vader Trivia Quiz}} *{{InsiderCite|37|Something Wicked This Way Comes}} *''[[Star Wars: Attack of the Clones Scrapbook]]'' *''[[Star Wars: The Complete Vader]]'' *[[Star Wars: The Skywalker Saga – The Official Collector's Edition|''Star Wars: The Skywalker Saga'' – The Official Collector's Edition]] *''[[The Star Wars Archives: Episodes I–VI, 1977–2005]]'' ==Notes and references== {{Reflist|2}} ==External links== *{{WP|Sebastian Shaw (actor)|Sebastian Shaw}} *{{IMDb|name/nm0789970}} {{Interlang |de=Sebastian Shaw |es=Sebastian Shaw |fi=Sebastian Shaw |ko=세바스찬 쇼 |nl=Sebastian Shaw |pl=Sebastian Shaw |pt=Sebastian Shaw }} [[Category:Actors who have portrayed Anakin Skywalker]] [[Category:Deceased actors]] [[Category:Star Wars: Episode VI Return of the Jedi actors]]
